input.prompt: Audit the project at the current working directory per your forensic method. Establish ground truth from git + the decomposed JSON + .context, diff the maintained records against it, attribute every discrepancy via claude-history, and return your structured report. Read-only — change nothing.

input.command: git status --porcelain && echo "---HEAD---" && git rev-parse HEAD && echo "---HEADTIME---" && git log -1 --format='%H %cI'
cat tmp/context-audits/.watermark.json 2>&1 || echo "NO_WATERMARK"
git log --pretty='%h %ad %s' --date=short -30
git diff prompt-workshop/dispatch/verify-citability.py | head -80
node context-migration/scripts/state.mjs filter ORCHESTRATOR-STATE.pending-actions.json status eq open 2>&1
node context-migration/scripts/state.mjs tail ORCHESTRATOR-LOG.json 2>&1
node context-migration/scripts/state.mjs tail ORCHESTRATOR-STATE.subagent-invocations.json 2>&1
git log d37573b~1..d5c9411 --stat --pretty='COMMIT %h %ad %s' --date=short 2>&1 | head -90
pi-context context-current-state --cwd . 2>&1 | head -60
pi-context context-validate --cwd . 2>&1 | tail -20

pi-context context-validate --cwd . --json 2>&1 | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print('NO json envelope')" 2>/dev/null; pi-context context-validate --cwd . 2>&1 | grep -E '"severity"|errors|status|valid' | head -30

pi-context context-validate --cwd . --json 2>&1 | python3 -c "
import sys,json
d=json.load(sys.stdin)
o=d.get('output',d)
issues=o.get('issues',[])
errs=[i for i in issues if i.get('severity')=='error']
warns=[i for i in issues if i.get('severity')=='warning']
print('status:',o.get('status'))
print('errors:',len(errs),'warnings:',len(warns))
for e in errs: print('ERR',e.get('code'),e.get('field'))
"
input.description: Parse validate JSON envelope for error count

input.command: pi-context filter-block-items --block tasks --field id --op eq --value TASK-057 --cwd . 2>&1 | head -50

pi-context filter-block-items --block tasks --field id --op eq --value TASK-056 --cwd . 2>&1 | python3 -c "import sys,json;d=json.load(sys.stdin);[print(t['id'],t['status'],'|',t.get('description','')[:90]) for t in d]"
pi-context filter-block-items --block tasks --field status --op eq --value in-progress --cwd . 2>&1 | python3 -c "import sys,json;d=json.load(sys.stdin);print('IN-PROGRESS:',[t['id'] for t in d])"
pi-context filter-block-items --block tasks --field status --op eq --value planned --cwd . 2>&1 | python3 -c "import sys,json;d=json.load(sys.stdin);print('PLANNED:',[t['id'] for t in d])"
pi-context filter-block-items --block tasks --field status --op eq --value completed --cwd . 2>&1 | python3 -c "import sys,json;d=json.load(sys.stdin);print('COMPLETED count:',len(d))"
pi-context find-references --id TASK-057 --cwd . 2>&1 | head -40

node context-migration/scripts/state.mjs filter seed-round-plan.json id eq DEC-56 2>&1 | head -30
echo "===DEC-55==="
node context-migration/scripts/state.mjs filter seed-round-plan.json id eq DEC-55 2>&1 | head -20
claude-history git-log 2>&1 | grep -iE 'TASK-057|TASK-056|verify-citab|DEC-56|DEC-55|citability' | head -20
claude-history file-history --path ORCHESTRATOR-LOG.json --project wasc 2>&1 | tail -15
claude-history file-history --path ORCHESTRATOR-STATE.pending-actions.json --project wasc 2>&1 | tail -15

claude-history file-history ORCHESTRATOR-LOG.json --project wasc 2>&1 | tail -12
echo "===PENDING==="
claude-history file-history ORCHESTRATOR-STATE.pending-actions.json --project wasc 2>&1 | tail -12

claude-history search "TASK-057 verify-citability" --project wasc 2>&1 | head -20
echo "===SESSIONS recent==="
claude-history sessions --project wasc 2>&1 | tail -8
claude-history search "head-accountability division-head leader Position" 2>&1 | head -15
git show 6d04799 --stat 2>&1 | head -20
claude-history search "citability invariant" 2>&1 | grep -v 'INFO\|daemon' | head -20

node context-migration/scripts/state.mjs filter ORCHESTRATOR-LOG.json refs contains TASK-057 2>&1 | head -5
echo "===LOG refs TASK-056==="
node context-migration/scripts/state.mjs filter ORCHESTRATOR-LOG.json refs contains TASK-056 2>&1 | head -5

input.command: claude-history execute_sql "SELECT a.agent_id, a.created_at, substr(a.description,1,70) FROM agents a JOIN sessions s ON a.session_id=s.session_id WHERE s.session_id='bd501b6f' AND a.created_at >= '2026-06-19 20:00' ORDER BY a.created_at" 2>&1 | grep -v 'INFO\|daemon' | head -30
input.description: List agents dispatched in the 2026-06-20 work window

input.command: claude-history --help 2>&1 | grep -iE 'agent|query' | head

claude-history query --session bd501b6f --tool Task --since "2026-06-19" 2>&1 | grep -v 'INFO\|daemon' | python3 -c "
import sys,json
try:
  d=json.load(sys.stdin)
except: 
  print('no json'); sys.exit()
rows=d if isinstance(d,list) else d.get('messages',d.get('results',[]))
print('Task dispatches since 2026-06-19:',len(rows))
for r in rows[:20]:
  ts=r.get('timestamp',r.get('created_at',''))
  print(ts)
" 2>&1 | head -30
claude-history query --help 2>&1 | grep -vE 'INFO|daemon' | head -40

claude-history query --session-id bd501b6f --tool Task --after "2026-06-19" --limit 50 2>&1 | grep -vE 'INFO|daemon' | python3 -c "
import sys,json
d=json.load(sys.stdin)
rows=d if isinstance(d,list) else d.get('messages',d.get('results',[]))
print('Task dispatches since 2026-06-19:',len(rows))
for r in rows:
  print(r.get('timestamp',''),'|',str(r.get('content',r.get('text','')))[:80])
" 2>&1 | head -40

claude-history query --session-id bd501b6f --after "2026-06-19 20:00" --limit 200 2>&1 | grep -vE 'INFO|daemon' | python3 -c "
import sys,json
d=json.load(sys.stdin)
rows=d if isinstance(d,list) else d.get('messages',d.get('results',[]))
tools={}
for r in rows:
  t=r.get('tool') or r.get('tool_name')
  if t: tools[t]=tools.get(t,0)+1
print('msg count:',len(rows))
print('tools:',tools)
" 2>&1 | head

node context-migration/scripts/state.mjs filter ORCHESTRATOR-STATE.pending-actions.json status eq open 2>&1 | grep -iE 'TASK-056|TASK-057|FGAP-046|FGAP-047|DEC-55|DEC-56|citability|head-accountab|seed-leader' | head
echo "EXIT_GREP=$?"
echo "===closing git status==="
git status --porcelain

input.content:
**WASC school-wide-improvement-plan — HEAD d5c9411 — audit ts 2026-06-20T09:50:40+08:00 — window FULL (baseline re-established: watermark `last_clean_head` is null) — prior `last_audit_ts` 2026-06-14T06:47:01Z (verdict FINDINGS; watermark never advanced to clean)**

The watermark's `last_clean_head` is `null`, which forces a FULL audit (a baseline that cannot be confirmed is the same as no baseline). The fast/incremental paths were not eligible.

---

1. **Finding** — The entire 2026-06-20 work day (two settled DECs, two framework-gaps, two tasks, and 5 commits of implementation incl. a Django migration and a gate-guard build) is absent from the event spine. The newest `ORCHESTRATOR-LOG.json` row and newest `subagent-invocations.json` row are both 2026-06-19; nothing records the 2026-06-20 events.
- **Artifact + field** — `context-migration/decomposed/ORCHESTRATOR-LOG.json` (405 rows, newest `timestamp` 2026-06-19T11:10) and `context-migration/decomposed/ORCHESTRATOR-STATE.subagent-invocations.json` (49 rows, newest 2026-06-19T11:05).
- **True value** — git shows 5 state-changing commits on 2026-06-20 with no matching LOG/invocations row: `d37573b` (file DEC-56+FGAP-047+TASK-057), `077616c` (propose-responsibilities output.schema), `e2afd5b` (conform 5 snippets+fragments to the DEC-56 guard, wire into gate), `7298dd5` (AST/dataflow tracer rebuild of verify-citability), `d5c9411` (clear 3 TASK-057 round-2 audit findings); plus the TASK-056 chain `6d04799`/`d4d1d5d`/`200535e` (DEC-55+FGAP-046, school migration 0062). `filter ORCHESTRATOR-LOG.json refs contains TASK-057` → `0 of 405`; same for TASK-056. The "round-2 audit findings cleared" commit message evidences IMPL + adversarial-audit agent dispatches that are absent from subagent-invocations.
- **Forensic attribution** — `claude-history search "citability invariant"` places all this work in session `bd501b6f`, with activity timestamps 2026-06-20T01:54 (DEC settling) through 2026-06-20T10:33; the same session committed `d37573b`..`d5c9411`. That session updated the seed-round-plan and `.context` records but left the LOG and subagent-invocations at their 2026-06-19 state.
- **Mandate violated** — CLAUDE.md: "The event spine is `ORCHESTRATOR-LOG.json` — append a row via `state.mjs append` for each state-changing event"; "append the … dispatch to `subagent-invocations.json` … after every subagent invocation"; "Verify, don't narrate: after every context update, RE-RUN the session-start queries … and confirm they reconstruct the reality you just created."

2. **Finding** — The seq-6 focus item (the top-level orientation surface) is stale: its head tag, `progress`, `next_step`, and `watch` describe the T6/distillation arc through 2026-06-19 and make zero mention of the 2026-06-20 citability-invariant arc (DEC-56/FGAP-047/TASK-057) or the head-accountability arc (DEC-55/FGAP-046/TASK-056) — the work that is actually active, on the very branch this checkout sits on.
- **Artifact + field** — `context-migration/decomposed/ORCHESTRATOR-STATE.pending-actions.json`, seq-6 `item` lead `[PHASE 2026-06-14: ENCODING the simulation findings into the workshopping]` and `next_step` (ends at the QUALITY-remainder / distiller, branch `t6-run` MERGED 2026-06-17). Grep of all open pending-actions for `TASK-056|TASK-057|FGAP-046|FGAP-047|DEC-55|DEC-56|citability|head-accountab|seed-leader` → no matches.
- **True value** — the live frontier is two new in-flight efforts: TASK-056 (school migration 0062 committed `6d04799`) and TASK-057 (verify-citability guard, committed `e2afd5b`/`7298dd5`/`d5c9411`, with an uncommitted mid-loop change still on the tree). The current branch is `seed-leader-head-accountability` (TASK-056's branch per DEC-55). DEC-55/DEC-56 are landed in `seed-round-plan.json`.
- **Forensic attribution** — session `bd501b6f` (`claude-history search "citability invariant"`, hits 2026-06-20T01:54–10:33) settled both DECs and committed both arcs while leaving seq-6's narrative fields pointed at the 2026-06-19 state.
- **Mandate violated** — CLAUDE.md: "A fresh session reads the focus item for both *which* work is active and *where-in-the-arc + what-is-next* … Keep these current as part of the same maintain discipline"; "refreshed at every decision boundary, after every subagent invocation, after every user direction that resolves an open item."

3. **Finding** — TASK-057 and TASK-056 are both `status: planned` in `.context` with no `verification_verifies_item` edge, while git shows both implemented (TASK-056 a committed migration; TASK-057 committed across 3 commits with an audit-cleared loop). The `.context` task statuses contradict the git ground truth of the work having been built.
- **Artifact + field** — `.context/tasks.json`: `TASK-056` `status: planned`, `TASK-057` `status: planned` (`filter-block-items --block tasks --field status --op eq --value planned` → `[TASK-037, TASK-056, TASK-057]`). `find-references --id TASK-057` returns only `task_addresses_gap`→FGAP-047 and `story_contains_task`→STORY-011; no verification edge.
- **True value** — TASK-056's build is committed (`6d04799`, migration `school/0062_seed_leader_head_accountability`); TASK-057's guard is committed and its round-2 audit findings cleared (`d5c9411`). Per the canonical pipeline's post-impl cascade, a built+audited task should carry an updated status + a `verification_verifies_item` edge. NOTE: `context-validate` is 0 errors / 5 warnings only because these tasks are still `planned` (the `completed-task-has-verification` invariant does not fire on a non-completed task) — so the missing cascade is invisible to the validator; this is a maintenance gap, and whether TASK-057's status should be `completed` depends on whether the uncommitted verify-citability change (finding 4) is the final loop iteration.
- **Forensic attribution** — session `bd501b6f` filed both tasks as `planned` at 2026-06-20T01:54+ (`d37573b`, `200535e`/`d4d1d5d`) and committed the implementations the same day without running the post-impl status cascade in `.context`.
- **Mandate violated** — CLAUDE.md canonical-pipeline step 5: "update the `.context` `task` status, add the `verification_verifies_item` edge … run `context-validate` so the cascade … is machine-verified not narrated"; "A completed task with no verification edge fails `context-validate`."

4. **Finding** — Uncommitted change on the working tree (`prompt-workshop/dispatch/verify-citability.py`) with no context record of the intentional mid-work dirtiness.
- **Artifact + field** — `git status --porcelain` → `M prompt-workshop/dispatch/verify-citability.py` (at both audit start and end). The diff reverts the paren-depth tracer from cross-line carry to per-line reset ("fail-safe … cannot HIDE a violation").
- **True value** — HEAD `d5c9411` ("clear the 3 TASK-057 round-2 audit findings F1/F2/F4") is the last commit on TASK-057; this uncommitted edit is a further change to the same file, i.e. an in-progress loop iteration. CLAUDE.md permits intentional mid-work dirtiness but requires it be explained in the context record (a LOG row + the focus item).
- **Forensic attribution** — the file's last commit is `d5c9411` (session `bd501b6f`, 2026-06-20); the working-tree edit post-dates it within the same session. No LOG row or focus-item note records an open/uncommitted state on verify-citability.
- **Mandate violated** — CLAUDE.md: "intentional mid-work dirtiness (an uncommitted coupled change, a staged input file) must be explained in the context record (a LOG row + the focus item), never left unexplained"; "You never leave the working tree dirty."

---

**What a fresh session would NOT know.** Orienting from the focus item (seq-6) + LOG + subagent-invocations, a fresh session would conclude the project is parked at the post-T6 QUALITY remainder (language-polish + distiller) as of 2026-06-19, with branch `t6-run` merged and nothing in flight. It would not know that: (a) two new decisions (DEC-55 division-head accountability, DEC-56 spec-citability invariant) were settled 2026-06-20; (b) two arcs were built that day — a Django seed migration (school/0062, TASK-056) and a 14-spec citability guard wired into the gate (TASK-057); (c) it is sitting on branch `seed-leader-head-accountability` mid-arc; (d) `verify-citability.py` has an uncommitted change representing an open loop iteration. The DEC layer (seed-round-plan.json) and the `.context` item-filing layer ARE current; the event spine, the focus narrative, and the `.context` status-cascade layers are a full work-day stale.

**VERDICT** — STALE / INCOMPLETE (4 findings).

**Read-only confirmation** — start `git status --porcelain` = ` M prompt-workshop/dispatch/verify-citability.py`; end `git status --porcelain` = ` M prompt-workshop/dispatch/verify-citability.py`. Byte-identical; this audit modified nothing.

Currency findings: 4

input.query: SELECT a.agent_id, a.subagent_type, substr(a.description,1,80) AS descr, a.created_at FROM agents a WHERE a.created_at >= '2026-06-20' ORDER BY a.created_at;
SELECT tool_name, substr(tool_input,1,160) AS inp, timestamp FROM tool_executions WHERE tool_name='Task' AND timestamp >= '2026-06-20' ORDER BY timestamp;

SELECT te.tool_use_id, substr(te.input_json,1,220) AS inp, m.timestamp FROM tool_executions te JOIN messages m ON m.uuid=te.message_uuid WHERE te.tool_name='Task' AND m.timestamp >= '2026-06-19T16:00:00Z' ORDER BY m.timestamp;

SELECT te.tool_name, substr(te.input_json,1,200) AS inp, m.timestamp FROM tool_executions te JOIN messages m ON m.uuid=te.message_uuid WHERE m.session_id LIKE 'bd501b6f%' AND te.tool_name='Task' ORDER BY m.timestamp;
SELECT DISTINCT session_id, min(timestamp) mn, max(timestamp) mx, count(*) c FROM messages WHERE timestamp >= '2026-06-19T15:00:00Z' GROUP BY session_id ORDER BY mn;

SELECT te.tool_name, substr(te.input_json,1,170) AS inp, m.timestamp, substr(m.session_id,1,8) sess FROM tool_executions te JOIN messages m ON m.uuid=te.message_uuid WHERE te.tool_name='Task' AND m.timestamp >= '2026-06-19T16:00:00Z' AND m.timestamp <= '2026-06-20T03:00:00Z' ORDER BY m.timestamp;

SELECT te.tool_name, count(*) c FROM tool_executions te JOIN messages m ON m.uuid=te.message_uuid WHERE m.timestamp >= '2026-06-19T16:00:00Z' AND m.timestamp <= '2026-06-20T03:00:00Z' GROUP BY te.tool_name ORDER BY c DESC;

SELECT m.timestamp, substr(m.session_id,1,8) sess, substr(te.input_json,1,300) AS inp FROM tool_executions te JOIN messages m ON m.uuid=te.message_uuid WHERE te.tool_name='Agent' AND m.timestamp >= '2026-06-19T16:00:00Z' AND m.timestamp <= '2026-06-20T03:00:00Z' AND (te.input_json LIKE '%citab%' OR te.input_json LIKE '%TASK-056%' OR te.input_json LIKE '%TASK-057%' OR te.input_json LIKE '%head-account%' OR te.input_json LIKE '%accountability%' OR te.input_json LIKE '%verify-citability%' OR te.input_json LIKE '%division-head%') ORDER BY m.timestamp;

SELECT m.timestamp, substr(te.input_json,12,40) AS descr, substr(te.result_content, -400) AS tail FROM tool_executions te JOIN messages m ON m.uuid=te.message_uuid WHERE te.tool_name='Agent' AND m.timestamp >= '2026-06-19T17:00:00Z' AND m.timestamp <= '2026-06-20T03:00:00Z' AND (te.input_json LIKE '%audit TASK-056%' OR te.input_json LIKE '%Re-audit guard round-2%') ORDER BY m.timestamp;

The 2026-06-20 context-currency audit (tmp/context-audits/audit-20260620T024735Z.md, 4 findings) found the whole 2026-06-20 work day absent from the maintained records: the event spine (ORCHESTRATOR-LOG), the dispatch record (subagent-invocations), and the seq-6 focus item were all a full work-day stale (newest rows 2026-06-19), and the .context TASK-056/057 statuses sat at `planned` while git showed both built. This records that work from git + claude-history ground truth. No product code changed; verify-citability.py is intentionally left uncommitted (see finding 4 below).

Spine (decomposed JSON):
- ORCHESTRATOR-LOG.json (+2 rows): the DEC-55/FGAP-046/TASK-056 head-accountability arc (school migration 0062, IMPL a30154e3 -> adversarial audit a2ca0cc2 Total findings: 0), and the DEC-56/FGAP-047/TASK-057 citability-invariant arc (AST/dataflow guard over 14 specs across e2afd5b/7298dd5/d5c9411), recording the OPEN iterate-to-zero loop (round-2 re-audit a2598e12 returned Total findings: 1 on F4's cross-line paren-depth soundness).
- subagent-invocations.json (+2 rows): the agent chains for both arcs, with agentIds from claude-history session bd501b6f.
- pending-actions.json seq-6: head tag + item NEXT + next_step CURRENT block + progress + watch brought current to the two 2026-06-20 arcs; the `watch` field now explains the intentional working-tree dirtiness on verify-citability.py (finding 4).

.context cascade:
- TASK-056 planned -> in-progress. The migration is built and adversarially audited to zero, with acceptance criteria 1/2/4/5 verified by audit a2ca0cc2 (scope: 13 division-head seats carry the atom, 4 year-group leaders + 30 staff carry 0; idempotent + reversible; grounding renders + parser admits the position-grain citation; gate green). Criterion 3 (a full clean 42-step workshop run completes without the spec-07 abort) is NOT yet demonstrated end-to-end and is jointly blocked on the open TASK-057; no verification entry is created, so the task is not completed.
- TASK-057 planned -> in-progress. The guard is built + wired into `make test-citability`, but the iterate-to-zero loop is open on the re-audit's paren-soundness finding; no verification entry, not completed.
- context-validate: 0 errors, 5 pre-existing warnings (unchanged from the audit baseline). The `completed-task-has-verification` invariant does not fire because neither task is marked completed — the statuses reflect the true unfinished state rather than a fabricated completion.

Aim: make the on-file context reconstruct the actual 2026-06-20 state for a fresh session, without asserting any completion or verification that did not occur.

SELECT COUNT(*) AS messages, COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS sessions, MIN(timestamp) AS first, MAX(timestamp) AS last FROM messages WHERE cwd LIKE '%wasc%'

SELECT CASE WHEN timestamp < '2026-05-29' THEN 'pre-workshop (5/14-5/28)' ELSE 'workshop-era (5/29-6/20)' END AS era, COUNT(*) AS messages, COUNT(DISTINCT session_id) AS sessions FROM messages WHERE cwd LIKE '%wasc%' GROUP BY era

SELECT CASE WHEN m.timestamp < '2026-05-29' THEN 'pre-workshop' ELSE 'workshop-era' END AS era, COUNT(*) AS agent_dispatches FROM tool_executions te JOIN messages m ON m.uuid=te.message_uuid WHERE m.cwd LIKE '%wasc%' AND te.tool_name IN ('Task','Agent') GROUP BY era

- A definitive yes/no: can the trio be built reusing only production primitives, touching zero prompt-workshop/pi-workflows/substrate code?
- The SINGLE place the most new code is needed.

Establish facts; do not recommend or pass a verdict.

input.args:
Comprehensive, CURRENT (June 2026) best practices and architectural options for LLM-based generation of structured content from explicit criteria + a closed corpus of authoritative data. HARD RECENCY CONSTRAINT: cite only sources from the last ~3 months (March–June 2026); discard anything older as stale; prioritize June 2026. Flag clearly if a claimed best practice rests on older work.

Our concrete problem (use to scope, but cover the general space): a system generates a large multi-section document (a school-improvement plan) by running ~14 dependency-ordered LLM calls, each producing one section, grounded in a tenant's ENUMERATED catalogue (the model may only cite values that exist in that closed set — no fabrication), each call's output validated by a parser before it's accepted, accumulating into one draft that must pass a structural "promotion gate." Today a single rejected call aborts the whole run; we want resilience.

Cover these dimensions, with named techniques, current tooling/frameworks, and trade-offs:
1. CONSTRAINED / STRUCTURED OUTPUT: current state of structured-output enforcement — JSON-schema / grammar-constrained decoding, tool-use-as-schema, provider structured-output modes (OpenAI/Anthropic/Google as of mid-2026), libraries (e.g. Outlines, Instructor, BAML, XGrammar or their 2026 successors). What's the current consensus on guaranteeing valid structured output.
2. CLOSED-CORPUS GROUNDING / ANTI-FABRICATION: how the field now enforces "use ONLY values from this enumerated set" — constrained decoding over a vocabulary, retrieval+citation verification, entity-linking, post-hoc validation+repair, groundedness/faithfulness checking. Distinguish open-web RAG from closed-catalogue constrained generation.
3. MULTI-STEP / SEQUENCED GENERATION of a long structured artifact with inter-section dependencies: current patterns — plan-and-write, decomposed/section-by-section generation, agentic orchestration, state accumulation across calls, context management. Frameworks people actually use now (LangGraph, DSPy, PydanticAI, etc. — current versions/status).
4. VALIDATION + SELF-CORRECTION LOOPS: generate→validate→repair, reflexion/self-refine, validator-in-the-loop, bounded-retry-with-error-feedback. Current best practice and known failure modes.
5. RUN RESILIENCE / PARTIAL-FAILURE ISOLATION: how production pipelines avoid whole-run aborts — per-step isolation, degrade-to-flag, checkpointing/resumability, idempotent retries. What durable-execution / workflow tooling is current (Temporal, DBOS, etc.) and whether it's warranted vs a plain loop.
6. EVALUATION of long-form structured generated content: LLM-as-judge (current rigor + pitfalls), rubric/criteria-based scoring, groundedness/faithfulness metrics, structural-completeness gates. What's trustworthy as of mid-2026.
7. ANTI-PATTERNS the field has converged AGAINST: over-engineered orchestration, brittle hard-abort gates, prompt-vs-schema drift, etc.

Deliverable: a comprehensive, cited, decision-oriented report — for each dimension the current recommended approach, the credible alternatives, and the trade-offs — so we can choose an architecture grounded in what the field actually does now, not 2024 practice.

angles.label: practitioner/implementation — multi-step orchestration + durable execution
angles.query: 2026 LLM multi-step pipeline orchestration LangGraph DSPy PydanticAI Temporal DBOS durable execution checkpoint resumability partial failure isolation
angles.rationale: Targets dimensions 3 and 5: current frameworks for sequenced long-artifact generation with inter-section dependencies, and durable-execution/workflow tooling for per-step isolation and avoiding whole-run aborts.

angles.query: 2026 LLM generate validate repair loop self-refine reflexion validator-in-the-loop bounded retry error feedback structured output failure modes
angles.rationale: Targets dimension 4: current best practice and known failure modes of generate-validate-repair, self-refine/reflexion, and bounded-retry-with-error-feedback for accepting or rejecting structured calls.

angles.label: academic/rigor — evaluation of long-form structured generation

angles.rationale: Targets dimension 6: trustworthiness of LLM-as-judge, rubric/criteria-based scoring, and groundedness/faithfulness metrics for long-form structured content as of mid-2026.

angles.rationale: Targets dimension 7 and cross-cuts all others: what the field has converged against (over-engineered orchestration, brittle hard-abort gates, prompt-vs-schema drift) via skeptical and production-experience sources to balance vendor hype.

Hard recency constraint: prioritize sources from the last ~3 months (March-June 2026), flag any claimed best practice resting on older work.
input.summary: Strategy: split the seven requested dimensions into five non-overlapping search angles, each pinned to 2026 (and where possible mid-2026) to enforce the recency constraint and to surface named techniques, current tooling/framework versions, and trade-offs. Angle 1 covers constrained/structured output + closed-corpus anti-fabrication (the decoding/grounding layer); Angle 2 covers multi-step orchestration frameworks and durable-execution resilience (the pipeline layer); Angle 3 covers validation/self-correction loops and their failure modes; Angle 4 covers evaluation of long-form structured content and LLM-as-judge rigor; Angle 5 hunts contrarian/anti-pattern and production-postmortem signal to counter hype. Each query embeds 2026 date anchors and named tools so results skew current and high-signal.

input.prompt: What are the key findings about LLM-as-a-judge reliability, biases, and assessment methodology? What date is this from and what does it recommend?

input.query: 2026 structured output constrained decoding pitfalls "encoding business rules in prompts" anti-pattern validation between steps closed vocabulary grounding postmortem
"Format Tax" OR ExtractBench 2026 constrained decoding accuracy degradation structured output trade-offs arxiv

input.prompt: What are the current 2026 best practices and techniques for LLM-as-a-judge, including rubric-based scoring, G-Eval, structural completeness, groundedness metrics, and validation against human labels?

What is the publication date? Summarize the core argument: why checkpointing in LangGraph/CrewAI/Google ADK is not durable execution, and what they recommend instead for partial-failure isolation and resumability.

What is the publication date? Summarize the recommended 2026 production architecture for durable LLM agent pipelines: per-step isolation, idempotency, replay safety, Temporal+LangGraph two-layer pattern, and when durable-execution tooling is warranted vs a plain loop.

input.query: 2026 self-correction LLM intrinsic verification limits external validator feedback "self-refine" reflexion when does it fail oracle

results.snippet: Directly contrarian: a vendor (Microsoft) arguing AGAINST agentic orchestration for known-structure work. Maps to dimension 7 and 3 — for a workflow with a fixed structure (the 14 dependency-ordered section calls), dynamic agentic orchestration 'adds cost, latency, and unpredictability.' Supports a deterministic backbone with LLM intelligence only at specific steps, which is exactly the current pipeline's shape. Use it to resist over-orchestrating the resilience fix.
results.title: Three tiers of Agentic AI — and when to use none of them (Microsoft, 2026)

results.snippet: Production-experience framing of the field's converged-against anti-patterns: 'context rot,' 'prompt drift' (a small change in step N cascades to N+1..N+5), 'resource exhaustion' in agentic loops, and the explicit warning that 'encoding business rules in prompts is an anti-pattern because rules expressed in prompt text drift as models update.' Directly hits dimensions 3, 4, 7 and the prompt-vs-schema-drift concern. Argues sequential-but-brittle pipelines (one failure stops everything) are the failure shape the user wants to escape.
results.title: LLM Workflows: Patterns, Tools & Production Architecture (2026) — Morph

results.snippet: Skeptical correction to structured-output hype (dimensions 1, 2, 7): constrained decoding guarantees syntactic validity but NOT semantic correctness — values can still be hallucinated or wrong even with perfect structure. Token-masking renormalization can produce 'syntactically correct but semantically degraded' output. The load-bearing distinction for the user's closed-corpus anti-fabrication goal: a guaranteed-valid response is not a guaranteed-correct one. Cites a benchmark where structured outputs REDUCED validity and accuracy vs prompt-based extraction. The durable rule: 'constrain the wire format, never the thinking.'
results.title: Builders beware: AI Structured Outputs are not all the same — Lak Lakshmanan (Medium)

results.snippet: Dimension 5 (run resilience / partial-failure isolation): explains why 'retry-the-whole-thing breaks at the agent boundary' and surveys current durable-execution primitives (Temporal, AWS Step Functions, Restate, DBOS, Inngest, LangGraph checkpointing). Frames the skeptical decision the user faces — when durable execution is warranted vs an in-process retry loop — rather than presenting it as a universal answer. Directly informs replacing the whole-run-abort with per-step isolation + checkpointing.
results.title: Durable Execution for LLM Agents 2026: Temporal + LangGraph (AppScale)

results.snippet: Dimension 5: production-oriented graceful-degradation playbook — keeping the pipeline operational on partial failure without aborting the user experience, fallback model swaps, and isolation. Supports the user's 'degrade-to-flag instead of abort the run' design direction (a single rejected call should not kill the run). Less skeptical/contrarian than the others but concrete on the resilience patterns the question asks for.
results.title: Fault Tolerance in LLM Pipelines: Key Techniques — Latitude

results.snippet: Postmortem-style coverage of cascading failure as the primary 2026 reliability bottleneck and 'agent drift' (progressive degradation over long sequences). Relevant to dimensions 3, 4, 5, 7: when you chain steps without validation between them, errors compound — reinforcing the user's parser-gate-each-step design and the case for validation-in-the-loop plus per-step isolation. Substack source, so weight the specific claims accordingly.
results.title: How Tool Chaining Fails in Production LLM Agents and How to Fix It — Future AGI

results.snippet: Dated June 6, 2026 — the most current source found, squarely on dimension 5. Lays out a 2026 production reference architecture for durable LLM agent pipelines: choosing among durable-execution primitives (Temporal, AWS Step Functions Express, Restate, DBOS, Inngest) layered with the LangGraph checkpointer model (PostgresSaver/RedisSaver/DynamoDBSaver), plus replay-safety contracts, idempotency-key derivation for non-idempotent tool/LLM calls, resumable streams, and HITL signal patterns. Directly addresses per-step isolation and avoiding whole-run aborts, and explicitly separates what durable execution fixes vs. doesn't.
results.title: Durable Execution for LLM Agents 2026: Temporal + LangGraph (AppScale Blog)

results.snippet: Dated February 25, 2026. The key decision-oriented argument for dimensions 5 and 7: checkpointing != durable execution. LangGraph/CrewAI/Google ADK persist state snapshots but push failure detection, resumption coordination, and duplicate-execution prevention onto the developer. Argues true durability needs runtime-integrated automatic retry/replay (cites Dapr Workflows/Agents). For a 14-call pipeline where one rejected call aborts the run, this directly informs whether a checkpointer alone buys resilience or whether a durable-execution runtime is warranted vs. a plain loop. Note vendor bias toward Dapr.
results.title: Checkpoints Are Not Durable Execution: Why LangGraph, CrewAI, Google ADK Fall Short (Diagrid)

results.snippet: Practitioner walkthrough of moving a working LangGraph graph to a durable runtime — the gap between in-memory checkpointing and a persistent, fault-tolerant store enabling resume-where-left-off, cross-node scaling, and audit history. Concretely relevant to converting today's abort-on-rejection loop into per-step-isolated, resumable execution. Pairs with the Diagrid critique on the limits of checkpointing alone.
results.title: Your LangGraph agent works. Now make the workflow durable. (ZenML Blog)

results.snippet: Official PydanticAI docs for the DBOS integration: a lightweight, library-style durable-execution layer (no separate orchestration server) that checkpoints workflow state to a database so on restart all workflows auto-resume from the last completed step. Strong candidate for dimensions 3 and 5 in a Django/Python stack — DBOS is in-process and Postgres-backed, a lighter alternative to a Temporal cluster for the team's ~14-call sequenced generation. PydanticAI also gives type-safe (Pydantic-validated) structured outputs, touching dimension 1.
results.title: DBOS Durable Execution — Pydantic AI Docs (official)

results.snippet: Explains the durable-execution programming model for agents: deterministic coordination logic runs/replays in the workflow while non-deterministic work (LLM calls, tool/API calls) runs once as Activities with results recorded in history, so expensive LLM calls are never lost or duplicated on crash/redeploy. Relevant to dimension 5's idempotent-retry and per-step-isolation question; Temporal is the heavier 'cluster' option vs. DBOS. Vendor source — weigh the Temporal-vs-plain-loop tradeoff against the team's modest scale.
results.title: Build Durable AI Agents with Pydantic AI and Temporal (Temporal)

results.snippet: Current side-by-side comparison of the main orchestration frameworks for dimension 3 (sequenced multi-step generation with inter-section dependencies). Useful for framework selection — graph-based LangGraph vs. type-safe PydanticAI vs. role/crew abstractions — and which pair with durable-execution backends. Buyer-guide tone, so corroborate specifics against the official docs above; verify the framework version/status claims against current releases.
results.title: Choosing an Agent Framework: LangChain vs LangGraph vs CrewAI vs PydanticAI vs Mastra vs Vercel AI SDK (Speakeasy)

results.snippet: Highly current and directly load-bearing for the architecture choice. Empirically shows grammar-constrained decoding raises JSON format-compliance from 55.7% to 92.2% but leaves task ACCURACY unchanged — most accuracy loss enters at the PROMPT (the format-requesting instruction itself), not the decoder constraint. Key recommendation: separate reasoning from formatting — generate freeform first, then reformat in a second pass (or use extended thinking) — to recover accuracy. For a per-section plan generator this argues against a single schema-constrained call per section and toward reason-then-format. Tested across 6 open-weight + 4 API models, 4 formats.
results.title: The Format Tax (arXiv 2604.03616, ~April 2026)

results.snippet: Current (2026) benchmark quantifying the limits of provider structured-output modes. Findings directly relevant to a multi-field validated pipeline: each provider's constrained-decoding mode supports only a SUBSET of JSON Schema, and unsupported schemas are a hard failure absent from prompt-based extraction; performance degrades with schema breadth, reaching 0% valid output on a 369-field schema across all tested models; and even when valid JSON is produced, extraction accuracy can still decline. Argues for decomposing wide schemas (which the ~14-call section-by-section design already does) and for keeping per-call schemas narrow.
results.title: ExtractBench: A Benchmark and Evaluation Methodology for Complex Structured Extraction (arXiv 2602.12247, ~Feb 2026)

results.snippet: Primary provider doc for dimension 1 (mid-2026 state). Documents Claude's two modes: JSON Outputs (validated JSON via output_format type json_schema, beta header structured-outputs-2025-11-13) and Strict Tool Use (guaranteed-valid tool inputs). Compiles JSON schema into a grammar that restricts valid tokens at each step so the model 'literally cannot produce tokens that would violate your schema.' Note: grammars apply to direct output, not tool results or thinking tags. Combined with the OpenAI (Aug 2024) and Google modes, establishes that native provider structured-output is GA across vendors by early 2026.
results.title: Structured outputs — Anthropic Claude API Docs

results.snippet: Directly addresses dimension 2 (closed-corpus / enumerated-vocabulary grounding). Explains the canonical anti-fabrication mechanism: given a predefined set of allowed outputs, constrained decoding dynamically restricts permissible tokens each step so the final output corresponds to EXACTLY ONE valid identifier from a closed set (product codes, URLs, legal terms, DB IDs). This is the technique that maps onto 'may only cite values that exist in the tenant catalogue' — distinct from open-web RAG. Covers minimally-invasive enforcement (Domino) to limit accuracy impact.
results.title: Beyond Free-Form Text: How Constrained Decoding is Reshaping Structured Generation in LLMs

results.snippet: Peer-reviewed 2026 work on the anti-fabrication problem for closed-source generation. The AEVS / anchor-constrained framework grounds every generated element back to the source text with provenance tracking to address LLMs hallucinating plausible-but-unsupported triplets. Relevant to enforcing 'cite only enumerated catalogue values' via entity-anchoring + provenance verification as a post-hoc validation layer complementing constrained decoding — i.e., the validate-and-verify side of the catalogue gate.
results.title: Grounded Knowledge Graph Extraction via LLMs: An Anchor-Constrained Framework with Provenance Tracking (MDPI Computers, 2026)

results.snippet: Current 2026 caution for dimensions 4 and 7 (self-correction loops, anti-patterns). Identifies 'structure snowballing' — an alignment tax where rigid constrained decoding during reflection/self-refine can lock in early errors rather than allow correction, a failure mode for generate->validate->repair loops that force schema constraints too early. Supports the Format-Tax conclusion: constrain format late, reason freely first; relevant to designing the validator-in-the-loop without brittle over-constraint.
results.title: From Hallucination to Structure Snowballing: The Alignment Tax of Constrained Decoding in LLM Reflection (arXiv 2604.06066, ~April 2026)

results.snippet: Recent (Dec 2025) academic critique directly addressing the trustworthiness question for dimension 6. Documents that LLM judges exhibit inconsistent reliability across assessment conditions, with verbosity bias, prompt-framing/context-ordering sensitivity, and temperature-dependent variation. Recommends moving beyond treating LLM judgments as absolute truth: explicit uncertainty quantification, confidence intervals on judge reliability, and robust benchmarking that accounts for judge variability. Borderline on the 3-month recency window (Dec 2025), but the most rigorous recent academic treatment of the core reliability question.
results.title: Are We on the Right Way to Assessing LLM-as-a-Judge? (Dec 2025)

results.snippet: Current (2026) practitioner-facing best-practice guide highly relevant to a parser-gated, criteria-driven pipeline. Names three concrete techniques: G-Eval (natural-language criteria with explicit evaluation steps), DAG (deterministic decision trees for hard gates / multi-step structural checks — directly analogous to a promotion gate), and QAG-style decomposition into closed-ended questions. Prescribes validating judges against human labels via the TP/TN/FP/FN matrix (FP = judge passes but human rejects is most dangerous) and using strict binary mode for gates. Maps cleanly onto rubric-based scoring + structural-completeness gating.
results.title: LLM-as-a-Judge in 2026: Top evaluation techniques and best practices (DeepEval)

results.snippet: Within the hard 3-month window (Apr 2026) and the most on-point rubric source. Frames rubrics as structured multi-dimensional scoring guides (factual accuracy, coherence, completeness, style, safety) and stresses negative criteria / pitfalls to catch frequent errors. Recommends starting from real failure modes, picking the right metric, writing a clear rubric, and validating against a golden dataset targeting 75–90% agreement with human labels. Directly supports criteria-based scoring of a long-form structured artifact against explicit per-section criteria.
results.title: Rubric-Based Evaluations & LLM-as-a-Judge — Methodologies, Biases, and Empirical Validation in Domain-Specific Contexts (Apr 2026)

results.snippet: Highly relevant to the closed-corpus/structured angle: evaluates LLM-as-judge against automated verification on specification-generation tasks and finds the judge produces unreliable verdicts that substantially overestimate specification correctness versus ground-truth verification. Strong empirical evidence for the report's anti-pattern dimension (don't trust an LLM judge where a deterministic verifier/parser exists) — corroborates the 'parser-gate is the real gate, LLM judge is advisory' design stance for structured output.
results.title: RESpecBench: How reliable is LLM-as-a-judge? Rigorous Evaluation of Specification Generation with Automated Verification

results.snippet: Reports a RAND-cited finding that no judge is uniformly reliable across benchmarks, with frontier models exceeding 50% error rates on challenging bias benchmarks and simple text-formatting changes disrupting consistency among judges that passed standard accuracy checks. Useful concrete magnitude for the reliability-pitfalls section and the case against relying on a single LLM judge for a promotion gate. Practitioner blog rather than peer-reviewed; date not firmly within the 3-month window.
results.title: LLM-as-a-Judge: Why Frontier Models Fail 50%+ Bias Tests

results.snippet: Addresses the groundedness/faithfulness metric dimension for long-form generation: empirically studies how to measure whether long-form output is grounded in provided context, the gap between sentence-level and document-level groundedness, and limitations of surface metrics (ROUGE misses hallucinated content). Relevant to faithfulness checking of generated sections against the closed catalogue, though it predates the recency window (2024) — flag as foundational/older work rather than current best practice.
results.title: Groundedness in Retrieval-augmented Long-form Generation: An Empirical Study

results.snippet: Directly answers dimension 4's core question of WHEN self-correction works. Establishes the now-dominant consensus: intrinsic self-correction (no external feedback) frequently fails and can degrade output; reliable repair requires an EXTERNAL validator/oracle. Critically, it identifies that self-correction is most reliable for DECOMPOSABLE tasks where verification is easier than generation -- which maps directly onto a parser-validated, per-section generation pipeline. This is the load-bearing citation that justifies your parser-in-the-loop architecture over model self-grading. NOTE: TACL survey synthesizes work partly older than 3 months; treat as foundational consensus, not a March-June-2026-only source.
results.title: When Can LLMs Actually Correct Their Own Mistakes? A Critical Survey of Self-Correction (TACL)

results.snippet: May 2026. Formalizes generate-validate-repair as a closed-loop CONTROL system, framing bounded-retry-with-error-feedback as a feedback-control problem rather than ad-hoc retry. Directly relevant to designing your bounded repair loop with stability/termination guarantees (avoiding infinite or oscillating retries) -- the control-theoretic lens is the current rigorous framing for the loop you want to build.
results.title: CyberCorrect: A Cybernetic Framework for Closed-Loop Self-Correction in LLMs

results.snippet: June 2026 -- meets the hard recency constraint and is the single most actionable result for your repair-feedback design. Empirically argues that the repair HINT should be a TYPED structured payload (a recovery_feedback field) appended to error responses, not prose -- so the next LLM call consumes it directly. This is exactly the design choice for what your parser returns on rejection: a machine-consumable error object the repair retry can act on, versus a natural-language explanation. 'Structure beats verbosity' is the headline finding.
results.title: Self-Reflective APIs: Structure Beats Verbosity for AI Agent Recovery

results.snippet: May 2026. Concrete bounded-repair-loop architecture: a verifier extracts structured behavioral evidence, then the loop applies either localized repair or a corrected-formulation repair depending on the failure class. Maps onto distinguishing a localized fix (one bad citation) from a whole-section regeneration, and demonstrates per-step verification feeding targeted (not blind) retry -- a model for resilience over whole-run abort.
results.title: OptiLoop: Coordination-in-the-Loop Verification and Repair for LLM-Generated Optimization Agents

results.snippet: 2026 practitioner guidance on the validate-then-one-repair-retry pattern in production pipelines. Recommends bounded repair (validation + a single repair retry) over unbounded retry loops, and operationalizes partial-failure monitoring (alert when validation-rejection rate exceeds ~10%). Useful for the production-resilience and degrade-to-flag angle: when to repair, when to surface rejection as a signal rather than loop indefinitely.
results.title: LLM Structured Outputs: Schema Validation for Real Pipelines (2026)

results.snippet: March 2026 survey situating generate-validate-repair within the broader self-improvement landscape, including the known failure mode where a model corrects an externally-presented error but fails to correct the IDENTICAL error in its own prior output. Useful context for why an independent validator (your parser) must be the arbiter rather than the generating model judging itself -- reinforcing the separate-validator design.
results.title: Self-Improvement of Large Language Models: A Technical Overview and Future Outlook

input.prompt: Extract the publish date, author, and the key falsifiable claims about: when to use agentic AI vs simpler patterns, tiers of agentic systems, anti-patterns of over-engineered orchestration, structured output / workflow orchestration, durable execution, validation loops, and when NOT to use agents. Quote exact sentences.

Extract claims about LLM workflow patterns and production architecture relevant to: constrained/structured output enforcement, closed-corpus grounding/anti-fabrication, multi-step sequenced generation with inter-section dependencies, validation/self-correction loops, run resilience/partial-failure isolation, evaluation of long-form generated content (LLM-as-judge), and anti-patterns. Report any publish date or "2026" recency markers. Quote directly. Note whether this is primary research, vendor blog, secondary reporting, or opinion.

Extract the publish date and all concrete, falsifiable claims about fault tolerance techniques in LLM pipelines: retries, fallbacks, circuit breakers, checkpointing, partial-failure isolation, per-step isolation, idempotency, validation/repair loops, durable execution. Quote exact sentences for each claim. Note any specific tools, frameworks, or metrics mentioned.

Extract claims about: durable execution for LLM agents, Temporal vs LangGraph checkpointing, partial-failure isolation, per-step retry/resumability, whole-run abort avoidance, when durable-execution tooling is warranted vs a plain loop. Provide direct quotes for each claim. Also note the publish date and whether this is a vendor blog or independent.

Extract the publish date and the main falsifiable claims about: (1) why checkpoints in LangGraph/CrewAI/Google ADK are not equivalent to durable execution, (2) failure-isolation/resilience in multi-step agent workflows, (3) what durable execution provides that checkpointing does not, (4) any claims about retries, resumability, idempotency, exactly-once semantics. Quote the exact supporting text verbatim for each claim. Also note who published it and the date.

Extract: (1) publish date; (2) author/source type (research institution, vendor blog, opinion); (3) concrete falsifiable claims about why tool chaining / multi-step LLM agent pipelines fail in production, partial-failure isolation, error propagation, validation loops, retry strategies, checkpointing, and how to fix them. Provide direct quotes for each claim.

Extract claims about AI agent frameworks for multi-step LLM orchestration: LangChain, LangGraph, CrewAI, PydanticAI, Mastra, Vercel AI SDK. Focus on: state accumulation across calls, structured output enforcement, durable execution/resumability/checkpointing, sequenced/multi-step generation, validation loops, framework trade-offs and recommendations. Also report the publish date of the article and any version numbers cited.

Extract the publish date and the key claims about LLM structured outputs. Specifically: what differences does the author identify between providers' structured-output modes (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google/Gemini)? What does the author claim about whether structured output guarantees valid JSON/schema conformance? What pitfalls, failure modes, or anti-patterns does the author identify? What recommendations does the author give? Quote exact sentences.

Extract concrete, falsifiable claims about: DBOS durable execution integration with Pydantic AI; how durable workflows provide partial-failure isolation, checkpointing/resumability, idempotent retries, step-level durability for multi-step LLM pipelines. Capture exact quotes describing what DBOS does, how it integrates (decorators, durable steps), and any version/date info. Also note publish date if shown.

Extract: (1) publish date; (2) author/source type; (3) concrete falsifiable claims about durable execution for AI agents, Pydantic AI + Temporal integration, partial-failure isolation, retries, checkpointing/resumability, and multi-step LLM orchestration. Quote each claim verbatim.

Extract claims about: durable execution / workflow runtime for LangGraph agents; how production multi-step LLM pipelines achieve resilience and avoid whole-run aborts; checkpointing, resumability, partial-failure isolation, idempotent retries; comparison to Temporal/DBOS or plain loops; multi-step orchestration patterns. Provide direct verbatim quotes. Also note the publish date and author/source.

Extract the paper's title, authors, institution, publication/submission date, and abstract. Then extract concrete falsifiable claims relevant to: constrained/structured LLM output (JSON-schema/grammar-constrained decoding, the "format tax" effect on quality/accuracy), closed-corpus grounding/anti-fabrication, multi-step structured generation, validation/self-correction loops, run resilience, and evaluation of structured generation. For each claim give the exact supporting quote and any quantitative findings (numbers, percentages, benchmarks).

Extract the title, authors, publication date, and abstract. What is this paper about? What are its main falsifiable claims regarding structured extraction, benchmarks, evaluation methodology, constrained/structured output, closed-corpus grounding, or LLM-based generation? Quote key sentences verbatim.

Extract concrete, falsifiable claims about: DBOS durable execution integration with Pydantic AI; how durable workflows provide partial-failure isolation, checkpointing/resumability, idempotent retries, step-level durability for multi-step LLM pipelines. Capture exact quotes describing what DBOS does, how it integrates (decorators, durable steps), and any version/date info. Also note publish date if shown.
Return the full body text of this article verbatim, including publish date and author.

Return the full body text of the article, especially any sections explaining: why LLM agents need durable execution, the replay-safety contract, idempotency keys for tool calls, Temporal vs LangGraph checkpointing comparison, resumable streams, and when to use durable execution vs a plain retry loop. Quote specific sentences with technical claims verbatim.

Extract claims about structured output enforcement: JSON schema support, strict/guaranteed valid output, tool-use schemas, grammar-constrained decoding, which models support it, how it guarantees valid structured output. Quote exact sentences. Note any publish/update date.

claims.claim: PipeBoost, a pipeline-parallel recovery system, cuts recovery time by 50.5%, reducing median recovery from 2.81 to 1.39 seconds.
claims.quote: PipeBoost: Cuts recovery time by 50.5%; reduces median recovery from 2.81 to 1.39 seconds

claims.claim: Checkpointing approaches divide into in-memory (fastest, memory-intensive), asynchronous (background writes during operations), and prioritized (critical states saved first) variants.
claims.quote: In-memory checkpointing (fastest but memory-intensive); Asynchronous checkpointing (background writes during operations); Prioritized checkpointing (critical states saved first)

claims.claim: Graceful degradation keeps basic services running during partial failures, e.g. switching to CPU-based processing or simplifying outputs when GPUs fail.
claims.quote: Keeps basic services running during partial failures.

claims.claim: Checkpointing regularly saves system state so the pipeline can restart quickly after a failure rather than aborting the whole run.
claims.quote: Regularly saves system states to quickly restart after failures.

claims.claim: TRANSOM, an asynchronous checkpointing system, reduces GPT-3 175B pre-training time by 28%.
claims.quote: TRANSOM: Reduces GPT-3 175B pre-training time by 28%

claims.claim: When a validation gate between workflow steps fails, the recommended pattern is to retry that step with the validation error injected into the prompt so the model can self-correct.
claims.quote: When validation fails, retry the step with the validation error in the prompt, giving the model a chance to self-correct.

claims.claim: Resilient production workflows enforce token-budget, step-count, and wall-clock limits, and when any budget is exhausted the workflow returns its best partial result rather than aborting wholesale.
claims.quote: Set a token budget, a step-count limit, and a wall-clock timeout. When any budget is exhausted, the workflow returns its best partial result.

claims.claim: Structured output modes (JSON mode, tool-call format) constrain model responses to parseable formats and remove an entire class of free-form-text parsing errors.
claims.quote: Structured output schemas (JSON mode, tool-call format) constrain model responses to parseable formats.

claims.claim: Multi-step LLM workflows decompose into six core orchestration patterns: Chain, Fan-Out/Fan-In, Map-Reduce, Router, Orchestrator-Worker, and Agentic Loop.
claims.quote: Six core patterns documented: Chain, Fan-Out/Fan-In, Map-Reduce, Router, Orchestrator-Worker, Agentic Loop.

claims.claim: Fully autonomous agents lacking a deterministic backbone are an anti-pattern because they behave unpredictably, alongside failure modes of context rot, prompt drift, and resource exhaustion.
claims.quote: Fully autonomous agents without deterministic backbone cited as unpredictable.

claims.claim: Temporal provides durable execution for AI agents that survives API failures, exceptions, application restarts, and deploys via replay-based fault tolerance, preserving in-flight progress and state.
claims.quote: Survive API failures, exceptions, app restarts, and deploys ... Preserve progress and state with replay-based fault tolerance

claims.claim: On a worker crash mid-run, Temporal replays the workflow without re-executing already-completed steps, giving per-step partial-failure isolation rather than a whole-run restart.
claims.quote: if your worker crashes after the dispatcher succeeds but before the researcher starts, Temporal replays the workflow, but it doesn't re-run the dispatcher

claims.claim: Non-deterministic work (model calls, tool executions, external API calls) is offloaded to Temporal Activities, which carry built-in retry policies automatically.
claims.quote: all the non-deterministic work (model calls, tool executions, external API calls) gets automatically offloaded to Temporal Activities with built-in retry policies

claims.claim: A Temporal-wrapped agent resumes from its last completed step after API timeouts or system crashes (checkpointing/resumability).
claims.quote: agent automatically picks up exactly where it left off, even after API timeouts or system crashes

claims.claim: Pydantic AI ships native Temporal support, enabling durable agents by wrapping an agent with TemporalAgent(), and can orchestrate multiple agents sequentially within a single workflow.
claims.quote: native Temporal support in Pydantic AI

input.prompt: Extract claims about structured output enforcement: JSON schema support, strict/guaranteed valid output, tool-use schemas, grammar-constrained decoding, which models support it, how it guarantees valid structured output. Quote exact sentences. Note any publish/update date.

claims.claim: Checkpointing in LangGraph/CrewAI/Google ADK is a low-level save-point primitive, not production-grade durable execution: it shifts failure detection, resumption triggering, and deduplication onto the developer.
claims.quote: Checkpointing is not production-grade durability. It's a low-level building block that shifts the hard problems onto you.

claims.claim: LangGraph provides no automatic failure detection — no supervisor, watchdog, or heartbeat — so a crashed process is not noticed by the framework.
claims.quote: No automatic failure detection. If your process crashes, no one knows. There is no supervisor, no watchdog, no heartbeat mechanism.

claims.claim: CrewAI checkpoint resumption lacks locking/deduplication, so concurrent resume attempts on the same flow can cause duplicate executions (no exactly-once guarantee).
claims.quote: if multiple processes attempt to resume the same flow, there's no locking or deduplication. You can easily end up with duplicate executions.

claims.claim: Durable-execution runtimes make every await point an automatic checkpoint and auto-restart on failure, replaying with completed steps returning cached results and only the failed step re-executing, requiring no manual intervention or external monitoring.
claims.quote: the runtime automatically restarted the workflow. On replay, [completed step] returns its cached result instantly, and [failed step] re-executes. No manual intervention, no external monitoring.

claims.claim: When LangGraph retries are exhausted the exception is simply raised — there is no built-in fallback routing, dead-letter queue, or notification system to degrade gracefully.
claims.quote: When retries are exhausted, the exception is raised. There is no built-in fallback routing, no dead-letter queue, no notification system.

input.prompt: List every FAQ question and any answer text present on the page. Also report the article author, publish date, read time, and any meta description or summary paragraph. Quote verbatim whatever text is present, even if it is only headings, FAQ questions, or summary blurbs.

claims.claim: Provider structured-output modes differ fundamentally in mechanism: Google Gemini uses constrained decoding (guaranteeing schema adherence), OpenAI uses a hybrid (constrained decoding with fallback to instruction-following), and Anthropic relies on instruction-following without constrained decoding.
claims.quote: OpenAI ... it seems to rely on constrained decoding...but seems to fall back on instruction-following if constrained decoding doesn't work.

claims.claim: Not all frontier models and agentic frameworks guarantee valid structured output, because not all use constrained decoding; some merely rely on the model following schema instructions.
claims.quote: not all frontier models and agentic frameworks use constrained decoding to support their JSON/XML/structured outputs.

claims.claim: Constrained decoding that strictly masks logits can FAIL to produce any output when the schema is very complex and no likely token continuation matches the schema.
claims.quote: if your schema is very complex and the none of the likely continuations match the schema — in such cases, the output generation will fail.

claims.claim: Relying on instruction-following (rather than constrained decoding) for schema conformance is riskier with faster/smaller models, which are weaker at instruction-following, making strongly-typed agentic frameworks built on such adherence unreliable.
claims.quote: I would be a bit apprehensive about building strongly typed agentic frameworks that rely on the model adhering to the given schema.

claims.claim: Builders should inspect each provider's actual implementation approach (via documentation) rather than assuming all 'structured output' features offer equivalent guarantees.
claims.quote: Builders, be careful out there

claims.claim: Validation (Pydantic or JSON Schema) should be inserted between every tool call in a chain to catch malformed output at the source before it propagates downstream.
claims.quote: Add input and output validation between every tool call using Pydantic or JSON Schema. Explicit validation catches errors at the source before they propagate.

claims.claim: LangGraph's durable-execution feature lets a broken chain resume from the exact failed step (e.g. step 4 of 7) instead of restarting the whole run.
claims.quote: durable execution feature means that if a chain breaks at step 4 out of 7, it can resume from that exact point instead of restarting

claims.claim: The dominant failure mode in chained LLM pipelines is silent error propagation: a malformed output from one call is consumed as a valid input by the next, so errors compound downstream without raising exceptions.
claims.quote: Your agent chains three or four tool calls together. The first call returns slightly malformed output.

claims.claim: Silent data corruption occurs specifically when a tool returns the wrong format and the agent forwards it undetected, motivating explicit format checks at boundaries.
claims.quote: Silent data corruption occurs when a tool returns the wrong format and the agent passes it forward undetected.

claims.claim: Inter-step context loss is a real failure mode: constraints stated early (e.g. a currency requirement) are lost in later calls because LLMs degrade on information buried in the middle of long contexts even with large windows.
claims.quote: LLMs lose performance on information buried in the middle of long contexts, even with large context windows.

claims.claim: No existing end-to-end benchmark evaluates PDF-to-JSON extraction under enterprise-scale schema breadth, which ExtractBench addresses with 35 PDF documents and 12,867 evaluatable fields across schemas ranging from tens to hundreds of fields.
claims.quote: no end-to-end benchmark evaluates PDF-to-JSON extraction under enterprise-scale schema breadth

claims.claim: Frontier models (GPT-5/5.2, Gemini-3 Flash/Pro, Claude 4.5 Opus/Sonnet) remain unreliable at producing valid structured output on realistic large schemas, producing 0% valid output on a 369-field financial reporting schema across all tested models.
claims.quote: frontier models (GPT-5/5.2, Gemini-3 Flash/Pro, Claude 4.5 Opus/Sonnet) remain unreliable on realistic schemas

claims.claim: All tested frontier models produced 0% valid output on a 369-field financial reporting schema, indicating that current structured-output enforcement does not guarantee validity as schema breadth scales.
claims.quote: 0% valid output on a 369-field financial reporting schema across all tested models

claims.claim: No principled evaluation methodology captures the semantics of nested extraction, where different fields require different notions of correctness (exact matching for identifiers versus semantic equivalence for names); ExtractBench treats schemas as executable specifications with field-specific scoring metrics.
claims.quote: no principled methodology captures the semantics of nested extraction

claims.claim: Multi-step LLM pipelines without a durable outer boundary recover from a mid-run step failure by restarting the entire run from scratch, which durable execution is meant to eliminate.
claims.quote: The graph may finish perfectly and the next step may fail. The report write times out...Without a durable outer boundary, the easiest way back is often to start the script again

claims.claim: A durable runtime (Kitaru) can wrap LangGraph by recording a workflow boundary around the graph while LangGraph manages internal graph state, separating durable orchestration from in-graph state management.
claims.quote: LangGraph controls the inside of the box. Kitaru records the durable workflow boundary around the box.

claims.claim: In the default mode, each completed graph invocation produces exactly one durable checkpoint, enabling downstream steps to retry independently rather than re-running the whole pipeline.
claims.quote: one completed graph.invoke(...) = one Kitaru checkpoint.

claims.claim: Durable workflows allow a run to resume on a different stateless worker after a worker disappears, but LangGraph's in-memory checkpoints require a persistent checkpointer backend for production restart semantics.
claims.quote: Pod A disappears...Pod B resumes the flow on Pod B

claims.claim: Side effects executed before an interrupt point must be idempotent (or relocated to safer boundaries) because durable replay can re-execute code up to the interrupt.
claims.quote: Side effects before `interrupt(...)` should be idempotent, or moved to safer boundaries

claims.claim: LangGraph provides durable execution via automatic checkpointing at every node transition (MemorySaver), so agents survive server restarts and can replay from any prior state.
claims.quote: LangGraph checkpoints at every node transition, so agents survive server restarts and replay from any prior state.

claims.claim: PydanticAI has no built-in persistence/durability layer; for durable execution it officially documents integration with external workflow engines Temporal, DBOS, and Prefect.
claims.quote: For durable execution, the framework officially documents integration with Temporal, DBOS, and Prefect.

claims.claim: The Vercel AI SDK has no native crash recovery or checkpointing and recommends integrating Inngest for durable execution.
claims.quote: There is no native crash recovery or checkpointing; Inngest is the recommended integration.

claims.claim: PydanticAI enforces structured output by automatically validating LLM output against Pydantic models, providing type-safety that the author credits with catching 23 bugs during development.
claims.quote: Full type safety and automatic LLM output validation against Pydantic models... The type system caught 23 bugs during development that would have reached production in LangChain.

claims.claim: The article's general orchestration recommendation is to use LangGraph for complex multi-step stateful workflows in Python (Mastra in TypeScript), framing the core trade-off as convenience (CrewAI) vs. control (LangGraph).
claims.quote: Multi-step stateful workflows in Python belong in LangGraph, and in TypeScript, Mastra.

input.prompt: Extract: (1) publish date, (2) author/credentials, (3) all concrete claims about constrained decoding, JSON-schema/grammar-constrained generation, structured output enforcement, tools/libraries (Outlines, XGrammar, Instructor, etc.), and any anti-fabrication/closed-vocabulary techniques. Quote key sentences verbatim.

claims.claim: DBOS provides durable execution by checkpointing program state in a database, so that on failure a restarted program automatically resumes its workflows from the last completed step.
claims.quote: DBOS makes programs "durable by checkpointing its state in a database. If your program ever fails, when it restarts all your workflows will automatically resume from the last completed step."

claims.claim: DBOS persists every workflow input and step output durably in its system database, providing the state-accumulation and step-level durability needed across multi-step LLM calls.
claims.quote: "Every workflow input and step output is durably stored in the system database."

claims.claim: DBOS separates deterministic workflows (no I/O) from steps (which may perform I/O such as network/API calls), and a failed step restarts from the beginning — isolating partial failures at the step level rather than aborting the whole run.
claims.quote: "Workflows must be deterministic and generally cannot include I/O. Steps may perform I/O (network, disk, API calls). If a step fails, it restarts from the beginning."

claims.claim: Pydantic AI's DBOSAgent automatically wraps Agent.run / Agent.run_sync as DBOS workflows and wraps model requests and MCP communication as DBOS steps, giving the integration its durability without manual orchestration of those calls.
claims.quote: `DBOSAgent` "automatically wraps `Agent.run` and `Agent.run_sync` as DBOS workflows" and "wraps model requests and MCP communication as DBOS steps."

claims.claim: Custom tool functions and event-stream handlers are NOT automatically wrapped by DBOS; if they perform I/O or are non-deterministic, the developer must explicitly decorate them with @DBOS.step.
claims.quote: "Custom tool functions and event stream handlers are **not automatically wrapped** by DBOS. If they involve non-deterministic behavior or perform I/O, you should explicitly decorate them with `@DBOS.step`."

input.prompt: Extract: (1) publication date / received-accepted-published dates; (2) the core technique of the "anchor-constrained framework" for grounded knowledge graph extraction via LLMs — how it enforces that extracted entities/relations come only from an anchor set / closed vocabulary; (3) provenance tracking mechanism; (4) any constrained decoding, post-hoc validation/repair, or anti-hallucination/faithfulness method described; (5) quantitative results comparing the anchor-constrained approach vs unconstrained baselines (hallucination rate, precision, faithfulness). Provide direct quotes verbatim for each.

claims.claim: Imposing structured-output format requirements (JSON, XML, LaTeX, Markdown) substantially degrades reasoning and writing accuracy across open-weight LLMs, averaging -5.8 pp (no grammar-constrained decoding) to -8.0 pp (with GCD), with 72 of 144 model-task-format cells showing statistically significant degradation (p<0.05).
claims.quote: structured output requirements—JSON, XML, LaTeX, Markdown—substantially degrade reasoning and writing performance across open-weight models

claims.claim: The accuracy cost of structured output originates at the prompt level (the format-requesting instruction itself), not at the decoder; grammar-constrained decoding adds little degradation, with 36 of 39 significant cells (92%) showing loss from the prompt alone.
claims.quote: format-requesting instructions alone cause most of the accuracy loss, before any decoder constraint is applied

claims.claim: Decoupling reasoning from formatting recovers most lost accuracy: two-pass generation (freeform first, then reformat) improves 42 of 72 comparisons by +6.8 pp average, and extended thinking improves 43 of 72 by +9.2 pp average.
claims.quote: generating freeform first and reformatting in a second pass...substantially recovers lost accuracy

claims.claim: Grammar-constrained decoding raises format compliance from 55.7% to 92.2% but does not improve reasoning accuracy (55.7% with GCD vs 57.3% without vs 61.5% freeform); producing valid structured output does not protect against accuracy loss.
claims.quote: models that produce valid structured output lose just as much accuracy as those that fail to comply

claims.claim: Recent closed-weight models show little to no format tax (claude-haiku-4.5 +1.2 pp, grok-4.1-fast +2.5 pp, gpt-5.4-nano +2.5 pp on average without GCD), indicating the degradation is not inherent to structured generation; the study covers only 3B-32B models and JSON/XML/Markdown/LaTeX formats.
claims.quote: recent closed-weight models show little to no format tax...suggesting the problem is not inherent to structured generation

input.prompt: Extract key claims about LLM-as-a-judge evaluation reliability, pitfalls, biases, and methodology. Specifically: what does the paper find about whether current LLM-as-a-judge benchmarks/practices are reliable? What concrete falsifiable findings, statistics, or recommendations does it make? Include the paper's title, authors, abstract, and publication date. Quote relevant passages verbatim.

Extract key claims about LLM-as-a-judge evaluation techniques and best practices for 2026. Focus on: evaluation methods (G-Eval, rubric/criteria-based scoring, groundedness/faithfulness metrics), known pitfalls and biases of LLM judges, structural completeness gates, and recommendations for evaluating long-form structured generated content. Include any publish date, exact quotes, and whether claims rest on recent vs older work.

claims.claim: Anthropic's Claude API enforces structured output via grammar-constrained decoding, compiling JSON schemas into a grammar that constrains the model's token sampling, yielding guaranteed schema-compliant responses without retries for schema violations.
claims.quote: Structured outputs work by compiling your JSON schemas into a grammar that constrains Claude's output.

claims.claim: Structured outputs guarantee schema compliance only in most cases; refusals (stop_reason refusal) and hitting the max_tokens limit are explicit scenarios where output may not match the schema, so guaranteed validity is not absolute.
claims.quote: While structured outputs guarantee schema compliance in most cases, there are scenarios where the output may not match your schema.

claims.claim: Anthropic exposes two distinct structured-output mechanisms: JSON Outputs via output_config.format for a specified JSON response format, and Strict Tool Use via strict:true to guarantee schema validation on tool names and inputs.
claims.quote: Strict Tool Use (strict: true): "Guarantee schema validation on tool names and inputs"

claims.claim: The Claude structured-output JSON schema support is limited: recursive schemas, numerical constraints (minimum/maximum/multipleOf), string length constraints (minLength/maxLength), and additionalProperties other than false are NOT supported, while enum/const/anyOf and array minItems (only 0 or 1) are supported.
claims.quote: Not Supported: Recursive schemas; Numerical constraints (`minimum`, `maximum`, `multipleOf`); String constraints (`minLength`, `maxLength`); `additionalProperties` set to anything other than `false`

claims.claim: Structured outputs are offered as eliminating the need for post-hoc validation/retry of schema conformance, branded as always-valid and type-safe with no JSON.parse errors and no retries needed for schema violations.
claims.quote: Always valid: No more `JSON.parse()` errors ... Reliable: No retries needed for schema violations

claims.claim: Grammar-constrained decoding can be made performant: XGrammar (2024) reportedly runs ~100x faster than prior grammar libraries via persistent parsing stacks, and DOMINO (ICML 2024) reports zero-or-negative overhead vs unconstrained decoding.
claims.quote: XGrammar (2024): Reported to achieve "100× faster than prior grammar libraries" through persistent parsing stacks ... DOMINO (ICML 2024): "Achieves zero or even negative overhead compared to unconstrained decoding"

claims.claim: Naive grammar-constrained decoding distorts the model's probability distribution, and Grammar-Aligned Decoding (ASAp) reweights so the model behaves as if trained with the grammar.
claims.quote: Grammar-Aligned Decoding (ASAp): Addresses probability distortion by reweighting so "the model behaves as if it was trained with the grammar"

claims.claim: For closed/proprietary APIs that lack token-level control, Sketch-Guided Constrained Decoding (ACL 2024) produces a draft JSON first then repairs it against the schema (a generate-then-repair pattern).
claims.quote: Sketch-Guided Constrained Decoding (ACL 2024): For proprietary APIs, "Produces a draft JSON first, then fixes mismatches against the schema"

claims.claim: Constrained/rigid output formats help classification tasks but can degrade reasoning-heavy tasks such as math and multi-hop QA.
claims.quote: rigid formats can hurt reasoning-heavy tasks (math, multi-hop QA), but help in classification tasks

claims.claim: JsonSchemaBench (2025) is a 10k-schema benchmark measuring structured-output validity, efficiency, and coverage.
claims.quote: JsonSchemaBench (2025): "10k-schema benchmark to measure validity, efficiency, and coverage"
